title: Neutropenic Sepsis
oneLiner: Life-threatening infection in cancer/immunosuppressed patients with low absolute neutrophil count (ANC <0.5×10⁹/L) – high mortality if antibiotics delayed.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette**  
A 62-year-old woman with relapsed acute myeloid leukaemia, day +10 post-consolidation chemotherapy. She feels “flu-like” since this morning, has a temperature of 38.7°C, heart rate 110 bpm, BP 92/58. She looks pale and tired. Her central line site is clean; no focal signs of infection. She has mild rigors. Her mouth shows scattered aphthous ulcers but no obvious thrush. Her last blood count showed neutrophils 0.2×10⁹/L, platelets 45×10⁹/L.

**Dissecting the Vignette – Pattern Clues**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Cytotoxic chemotherapy (day +10) | Nadir bone marrow suppression (neutrophil count lowest around day 7–14) |
| Neutrophils 0.2×10⁹/L | Profound neutropenia – ANC <0.5 defines neutropenia; <0.1 is severe |
| Fever (38.7°C) | Systemic inflammatory response – but may be only sign of infection |
| Tachycardia, hypotension | Sepsis – vasodilation from cytokines; early signs of distributive shock |
| Rigors | Bacteraemia (especially Gram-negative) or fungemia |
| No focal signs | Typical: neutropenic patients cannot mount pus – no chest crackles, no urinary symptoms, no cellulitis |
| Mouth ulcers | Potential portal for Gram-positive bacteria (viridans strep) or yeasts |

**Atypical Presentations That Trick You**  
- **Afebrile neutropenic sepsis** – elderly or on steroids: no fever, just confusion + tachypnoea + lactate rise.  
- **Hypothermic neutropenic sepsis** – temperature <36°C: indicates severe sepsis with poor perfusion; mortality higher than with fever.  
- **Abdominal pain only** – typhlitis (neutropenic enterocolitis) presents with right lower quadrant pain, diarrhoea, and can mimic appendicitis. No fever until late.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition**  
Neutropenic sepsis is a systemic inflammatory response to infection occurring in a patient whose absolute neutrophil count (ANC) is <0.5×10⁹/L. Because neutrophils are the body’s first-line defence against bacteria and fungi, even a trivial mucosal breach can cause overwhelming bloodstream infection. The key point: **fever in a neutropenic patient is a medical emergency – assume sepsis until proven otherwise.**

**Why it matters – frequency, stakes, pitfalls**  
- **Frequency:** Up to 80% of patients receiving intensive chemotherapy will develop at least one episode of febrile neutropenia.  
- **Stakes:** Mortality is 5–10% even with modern care; if antibiotics delayed beyond 1 hour, mortality rises significantly (up to 20–30%).  
- **Common pitfall:** Waiting for culture results or for the patient to “look septic” before starting antibiotics. **Do not wait** – neutropenic patients often have minimal signs until cardiovascular collapse.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of how it develops**

1. **Bone marrow suppression** – chemotherapy damages haematopoietic stem cells → neutrophil count plummets to <0.5×10⁹/L.  
2. **Loss of mucosal barriers** – chemotherapy also damages gut and oral mucosa → bacteria (especially Gram-negatives like *E. coli*, *Pseudomonas*) enter the bloodstream. Indwelling central lines provide another portal for skin flora (coagulase-negative staphylococci).  
3. **No neutrophil army** – normally, neutrophils migrate to infection sites and kill bacteria. Without them, bacteria multiply unchecked → rapid bacteraemia.  
4. **Cytokine storm** – macrophages and endothelial cells release TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6 → fever, vasodilation, capillary leak, hypotension (septic shock).  
5. **Organ hypoperfusion** – lactic acidosis, renal impairment, altered consciousness (sepsis-associated encephalopathy).

**Why each key clinical sign happens**

| Sign | Mechanism |
|------|-----------|
| Fever | Pyrogenic cytokines (IL-1, TNF) reset hypothalamic set-point |
| Tachycardia | Compensatory response to vasodilation ± hypovolaemia |
| Hypotension | Cytokine-induced vasodilation + relative hypovolaemia |
| Rigors | Rapid bacteraemia (especially Gram-negatives) |
| No focal signs | Absent neutrophils = no pus, no consolidation, no pyuria |
| Abdominal pain (typhlitis) | Bacterial invasion of cecal wall → inflammation without neutrophils → necrosis |

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Cause Bin | Common Triggers | Stem Clue |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Chemotherapy** (most common) | Cytarabine, anthracyclines, platinum, alkylating agents | “Day +10 post-chemo” |
| **Haematological malignancy** | AML, ALL, high-grade NHL, multiple myeloma | Pancytopenia at diagnosis (marrow replacement) |
| **Immunosuppressive therapy** | Tacrolimus, mycophenolate, anti-thymocyte globulin | Post-transplant patient on multiple immunosuppressants |
| **Autoimmune neutropenia** | SLE, Felty’s syndrome | Positive ANA, rheumatoid factor, splenomegaly |
| **Congenital** | Severe congenital neutropenia (Kostmann), cyclic neutropenia | Recurrent infections since infancy |
| **Drug-induced non-chemotherapy** | Clozapine (most notorious), carbimazole, sulfonamides, NSAIDs | Patient on antipsychotic or antithyroid drug |

**Most common cause:** Chemotherapy-induced neutropenia (especially AML/ALL induction). **Always ask:** “When was the last cycle? Day? What drugs?”

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (NICE / IDSA)**  
- **ANC <0.5×10⁹/L** AND  
- **Temperature ≥38°C** (single reading) OR ≥37.5°C sustained over 1 hour  
- *Note: Some guidelines use ANC <0.5 or <1.0† with fever as definition of febrile neutropenia. Severity correlates with ANC <0.1 (profound).*

**Approach: get blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics, but do not delay antibiotics more than 15–30 min.**

| Test | What It Shows / Why | Interpretation |
|------|----------------------|---------------|
| **FBC + differential** | Confirms neutropenia, assesses platelets, Hb | ANC <0.5 = neutropenia. Platelet <50 = risk of bleeding during central line access |
| **Blood cultures** (peripheral + from each lumen of central line) | Identifies pathogen – most common: Gram-positive cocci (staph, strep) ~60%, Gram-negative rods ~30%, fungi ~10% | Any growth is significant. **If central line culture grows 2h earlier than peripheral (differential time to positivity) = line infection** |
| **Urine culture** | Even without dysuria – low sensitivity because no pus → false negative. Still send if fever persists >48h | Positive may indicate occult urinary source |
| **Chest X-ray** | Look for consolidation (even if no cough/crackles) | Bilateral infiltrates suggest *Pneumocystis* or fluid overload; focal lobar = bacterial (but may be absent) |
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion marker | ≥2 mmol/L = occult hypoperfusion; ≥4 = high risk for septic shock |
| **CRP / procalcitonin** | Inflammatory marker | Procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL supports bacterial infection (less specific in neutropenia than in non-neutropenic sepsis) |
| **Serum galactomannan** | Neutropenic patients at risk for invasive aspergillosis | Positive ≥0.5 for bronchoalveolar lavage or serum = likely aspergillosis (especially if antifungal coverage failing) |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms?**  
- **Positive blood culture** confirms bacteraemia/fungemia, but sterile cultures do not rule out infection (30–50% are culture-negative).  
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** in a febrile neutropenic patient = severe sepsis until proven otherwise.

**What to repeat:** Blood cultures daily if fever persists beyond 48h. Consider CT chest/abdomen if no source found (occult typhlitis, abscess).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – “The Golden Hour”**  
- **IV antibiotics:** **Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV** (over 30 min) – covers *Pseudomonas*, *E. coli*, *Klebsiella*, anaerobes, and many Gram-positives.  
- **Why this choice:** Monotherapy is as effective as dual therapy (aminoglycoside + beta-lactam) for uncomplicated febrile neutropenia, with less nephrotoxicity.  
- **Alternative if penicillin allergy:** **IV meropenem 1 g IV** (but avoid if mild allergy; use cefepime 2 g IV or ceftazidime + amikacin).  
- **Fluid resuscitation:** **500 mL–1 L crystalloid** (Hartmann’s / 0.9% saline) over 15–30 min if hypotensive. Reassess.  
- **Blood cultures:** from peripheral vein AND each central line lumen – but **do not delay antibiotics**; can draw cultures while preparing infusion.  
- **Monitor:** BP, HR, temp, urine output, lactate every hour.

**Ward (24–72h)**  
- **Reassess:** If fever persists >72h or recurs after defervescence, escalate to **IV vancomycin** (if not already on it) – suspect MRSA or line infection. Also consider **IV antifungal** (caspofungin 70 mg load then 50 mg daily) if >96h of fever without source.  
- **Check cultures:** If positive, narrow antibiotics (e.g., if *E. coli* sensitive to piperacillin, continue same; if MRSA, add vancomycin and stop anti-Gram-negative coverage if not needed).  
- **G-CSF (filgrastim/lenograstim):** Not routine for established febrile neutropenia – use only if prolonged neutropenia (≥7 days) or pneumonia/hypotension. **Do not give in first 24h** – may worsen fever.  
- **Monitor ANC daily.** Once ANC recovers (>0.5 and rising), fever usually resolves.

**Long-term**  
- **Secondary prophylaxis:** If recurrent severe neutropenia, consider prophylactic antibiotics (levofloxacin) or prophylactic G-CSF for next chemotherapy cycle.  
- **Central line management:** If line infection confirmed (same organism from line and peripheral, differential time to positivity >2h), remove line after 48h of effective antibiotics; if line essential, lock therapy can be tried but removal is gold standard.

**Common Pitfalls – What NOT to Do**  
- **Don’t use aminoglycoside monotherapy** – poor Gram-positive coverage and nephrotoxic.  
- **Don’t rely on antipyretics** (paracetamol) to “treat” fever – they mask the temperature curve and do not treat infection. Only give if severe rigors/haemodynamic instability.  
- **Don’t give G-CSF immediately** – not beneficial in first 24h, may cause bone pain and increased fever.  
- **Don’t delay antibiotics for imaging** – CXR and CT can wait until after first dose.  
- **Don’t forget viral/fungal causes** – if no response after 48h, consider CMV, EBV, respiratory viruses, *Pneumocystis* (add TMP-SMX if high suspicion).  
- **Don’t assume afebrile = no infection** – especially in elderly or on steroids.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
The main mimic is **non-neutropenic sepsis**. But in a patient with fever and low WBC, also consider:

| Feature | Neutropenic Sepsis | Non-Neutropenic Sepsis | ***Clostridium difficile* Colitis** | ***Pneumocystis* Pneumonia** | **Typhlitis (Neutropenic Enterocolitis)** |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually absent (no pus) | Often focal (chest, abdomen, urinary) | Crampy abdominal pain, diarrhoea | Pleuritic chest pain | RLQ or periumbilical pain |
| **Exam** | No focal signs; may have rigors | Focal tenderness, crackles, costovertebral angle tenderness | Diffuse abdominal tenderness | Bilateral crackles (or none) | RLQ tenderness, guarding – but no rebound |
| **ANC** | <0.5 | Normal or elevated | Normal or low if sepsis | Normal or low | <0.5 |
| **Lactate** | Elevated | May be elevated | Normal | Normal | Can be elevated |
| **CXR** | Often normal | Consolidation, effusion | Normal | Bilateral interstitial infiltrates (ground-glass) | Normal; CT shows thickened cecal wall |
| **Key differentiating test** | Blood culture + low ANC | Blood culture + source found | Stool toxin positive | CT chest + bronchoalveolar lavage (PCR) + lactate dehydrogenase high | CT abdomen: cecal wall thickening >3 mm |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Septic shock** | Persistent hypotension despite 2L fluid, lactate >4 mmol/L, altered mental state, oliguria | MAP <65, skin mottling, capillary refill >3s | Start noradrenaline (titrate to MAP >65), escalate to ICU, consider hydrocortisone 50 mg IV QDS |
| **Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)** | Hypoxic respiratory failure with bilateral infiltrates on CXR | PaO2/FiO2 <200, crackles, confusion | Non-invasive ventilation or intubation; lung-protective ventilation; consider diuresis |
| **Invasive fungal infection** | High fever persisting >4 days despite broad-spectrum antibiotics, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis | CT chest: halo sign (aspergillosis) | Start voriconazole (6 mg/kg IV BD) or liposomal amphotericin B; consider bronchoscopy |
| **Neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis)** | Abdominal pain, bloody diarrhoea, fever, and right lower quadrant tenderness | CT shows thickened cecal wall, no free air | Broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam covers anaerobes), IV fluids, bowel rest surgery if perforation or bleeding |
| **Haemorrhage** | Thrombocytopenia (<20) plus thrombocytopenia-associated bleeding (epistaxis, petechiae, GI bleed) | Platelet count <20, signs of shock | Platelet transfusion 1 pool; treat coagulopathy; if central line bleeding, hold anticoagulants |

**Late-presentation patterns (the “untreated” stem)**  
- A patient with prolonged neutropenia (>14 days) who develops sudden pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, and a new cavity on CXR → **invasive pulmonary aspergillosis**.  
- A patient who remains febrile after 7 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics, with new abdominal distension and bloody diarrhoea → **typhlitis** (CT is diagnostic).  
- A patient with central line who develops rigors after line flushing, with positive blood cultures and no other source → **line-related sepsis** (remove line after 48h of antibiotics).

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: “FEVER” in a neutropenic patient**  
- **F** – Full septic screen (cultures, lactate, CXR)  
- **E** – Empiric antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam) **within 1 hour**  
- **V** – Vital signs monitoring (NEWS2)  
- **E** – Escalate if no response: vancomycin + antifungal  
- **R** – Re-assess after 72h: narrow if culture positive, add antifungal if persistent fever

**Mnemonic: “CHEMOTHERAPY” for causes**  
- C – Chemotherapy (most common)  
- H – Haematological malignancy  
- E – Immunosuppressants (e.g., tacrolimus)  
- M – Marrow replacement (leukaemia)  
- O – Other drugs (clozapine, carbimazole)  
- T – Trauma (rare)  
- H – Hypersplenism  
- E – Systemic lupus erythematosus  
- R – Rheumatoid (Felty’s)  
- A – Aplastic anaemia  
- P – Parvovirus B19 (transient)  
- Y – Young age (congenital)

**5–7 High-Yield Clinical Pearls**  

1. **“Fever + neutropenia = sepsis until proven otherwise.”** Do not wait for cultures – first dose of antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition.  
2. **No focal signs does not mean no infection.** Neutropenic patients cannot form pus. An afebrile, hypotensive patient can still have septic shock.  
3. **Lactate is your friend.** A normal lactate in a febrile neutropenic patient is reassuring; a rising lactate is the earliest sign of deterioration before BP drops.  
4. **Piperacillin-tazobactam is king** – it covers *Pseudomonas*, anaerobes, and most Gram-positives. Only add vancomycin if suspected line infection, MRSA colonisation, or skin/soft tissue source.  
5. **Persistent fever >96h = think fungus.** Start caspofungin or voriconazole empirically; consider CT chest and abdomen.  
6. **Don’t stop antibiotics early.** Continue until ANC recovers to >0.5 (or >1.0 if still febrile). Usually 7–14 days for bacteraemia.  
7. **G-CSF is not for acute management.** It is for prophylaxis before the next cycle. In established sepsis, the inflammatory cytokines already raise neutrophil count; G-CSF may worsen capillary leak.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid**  
- **Using co-amoxiclav instead of piperacillin-tazobactam** – co-amoxiclav has poor anti-pseudomonal activity.  
- **Ordering a procalcitonin and delaying antibiotics** – no single test is sensitive enough to rule out bacterial infection in neutropenia.  
- **Assuming afebrile = safe** – check lactate and BP on every patient with neutropenia, even without fever.  
- **Forgetting to draw blood cultures from the central line** – you need differential time to positivity to diagnose line infection.  
- **Giving paracetamol for temperature** – masks fever and may hide response to treatment.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (registrar / consultant)**  
- Any neutropenic patient with fever ≥38°C (or hypothermia <36°C) – this is already a sepsis call.  
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** despite initial fluids.  
- **BP <90/60 or MAP <65** after 500 mL fluid bolus.  
- **GCS <15** or new confusion.  
- **Respiratory rate >25 / SpO2 <92% on room air.**  
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** (see below).

**Specific thresholds for ICU/crash team**  
- **NEWS2 ≥7** – immediate ICU referral.  
- **Lactate ≥4 mmol/L** (severe sepsis / septic shock) – transfer to ICU for central line, vasopressors, invasive monitoring.  
- **GCS ≤12** – airway protection risk; consider intubation.  
- **Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 hours** despite adequate fluids.  
- **Refractory hypotension** – not responding to 2 L crystalloid + noradrenaline >0.5 mcg/kg/min.

**NEWS2 scoring (quick reference):**  
| Parameter | Score 0 | Score 1 | Score 2 | Score 3 |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| RR | 12–20 | 9–11 | 21–24 | ≤8 or ≥25 |
| SpO2 | ≥96% | 94–95% | 92–93% | ≤91% |
| Temp | 36.1–38.0 | 35.1–36.0 or 38.1–39.0 | ≥39.1 | ≤35.0 |
| SBP | ≥110 | 100–109 | 90–99 | ≤89 |
| HR | 51–90 | 41–50 or 91–110 | 111–130 | ≤40 or ≥131 |
| Consciousness | Alert | | | New confusion / not alert |

**Remember:** Neutropenic sepsis can deteriorate faster than other sepsis because the lack of neutrophils prevents localisation. Any abnormal vital sign in a neutropenic patient is a red flag. Escalate early, treat early, and never assume it’s “just a fever.”

**One-liner:** Life-threatening infection in cancer/immunosuppressed patients with low absolute neutrophil count (ANC <0.5×10⁹/L) – high mortality if antibiotics delayed.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

| Sign | Mechanism |

| Cause Bin | Common Triggers | Stem Clue |

- **Don’t delay antibiotics for imaging** – CXR and CT can wait until after first dose.  
- **Don’t forget viral/fungal causes** – if no response after 48h, consider CMV, EBV, respiratory viruses, *Pneumocystis* (add TMP-SMX if high suspicion).  
- **Don’t assume afebrile = no infection** – especially in elderly or on steroids.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

The main mimic is **non-neutropenic sepsis**. But in a patient with fever and low WBC, also consider:

| Feature | Neutropenic Sepsis | Non-Neutropenic Sepsis | ***Clostridium difficile* Colitis** | ***Pneumocystis* Pneumonia** | **Typhlitis (Neutropenic Enterocolitis)** |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually absent (no pus) | Often focal (chest, abdomen, urinary) | Crampy abdominal pain, diarrhoea | Pleuritic chest pain | RLQ or periumbilical pain |
| **Exam** | No focal signs; may have rigors | Focal tenderness, crackles, costovertebral angle tenderness | Diffuse abdominal tenderness | Bilateral crackles (or none) | RLQ tenderness, guarding – but no rebound |
| **ANC** | <0.5 | Normal or elevated | Normal or low if sepsis | Normal or low | <0.5 |
| **Lactate** | Elevated | May be elevated | Normal | Normal | Can be elevated |
| **CXR** | Often normal | Consolidation, effusion | Normal | Bilateral interstitial infiltrates (ground-glass) | Normal; CT shows thickened cecal wall |
| **Key differentiating test** | Blood culture + low ANC | Blood culture + source found | Stool toxin positive | CT chest + bronchoalveolar lavage (PCR) + lactate dehydrogenase high | CT abdomen: cecal wall thickening >3 mm |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Septic shock** | Persistent hypotension despite 2L fluid, lactate >4 mmol/L, altered mental state, oliguria | MAP <65, skin mottling, capillary refill >3s | Start noradrenaline (titrate to MAP >65), escalate to ICU, consider hydrocortisone 50 mg IV QDS |
| **Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)** | Hypoxic respiratory failure with bilateral infiltrates on CXR | PaO2/FiO2 <200, crackles, confusion | Non-invasive ventilation or intubation; lung-protective ventilation; consider diuresis |
| **Invasive fungal infection** | High fever persisting >4 days despite broad-spectrum antibiotics, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis | CT chest: halo sign (aspergillosis) | Start voriconazole (6 mg/kg IV BD) or liposomal amphotericin B; consider bronchoscopy |
| **Neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis)** | Abdominal pain, bloody diarrhoea, fever, and right lower quadrant tenderness | CT shows thickened cecal wall, no free air | Broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam covers anaerobes), IV fluids, bowel rest surgery if perforation or bleeding |
| **Haemorrhage** | Thrombocytopenia (<20) plus thrombocytopenia-associated bleeding (epistaxis, petechiae, GI bleed) | Platelet count <20, signs of shock | Platelet transfusion 1 pool; treat coagulopathy; if central line bleeding, hold anticoagulants |

**Late-presentation patterns (the “untreated” stem)**  
- A patient with prolonged neutropenia (>14 days) who develops sudden pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, and a new cavity on CXR → **invasive pulmonary aspergillosis**.  
- A patient who remains febrile after 7 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics, with new abdominal distension and bloody diarrhoea → **typhlitis** (CT is diagnostic).  
- A patient with central line who develops rigors after line flushing, with positive blood cultures and no other source → **line-related sepsis** (remove line after 48h of antibiotics).

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: “FEVER” in a neutropenic patient**  
- **F** – Full septic screen (cultures, lactate, CXR)  
- **E** – Empiric antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam) **within 1 hour**  
- **V** – Vital signs monitoring (NEWS2)  
- **E** – Escalate if no response: vancomycin + antifungal  
- **R** – Re-assess after 72h: narrow if culture positive, add antifungal if persistent fever

**Mnemonic: “CHEMOTHERAPY” for causes**  
- C – Chemotherapy (most common)  
- H – Haematological malignancy  
- E – Immunosuppressants (e.g., tacrolimus)  
- M – Marrow replacement (leukaemia)  
- O – Other drugs (clozapine, carbimazole)  
- T – Trauma (rare)  
- H – Hypersplenism  
- E – Systemic lupus erythematosus  
- R – Rheumatoid (Felty’s)  
- A – Aplastic anaemia  
- P – Parvovirus B19 (transient)  
- Y – Young age (congenital)

**5–7 High-Yield Clinical Pearls**  

1. **“Fever + neutropenia = sepsis until proven otherwise.”** Do not wait for cultures – first dose of antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition.  
2. **No focal signs does not mean no infection.** Neutropenic patients cannot form pus. An afebrile, hypotensive patient can still have septic shock.  
3. **Lactate is your friend.** A normal lactate in a febrile neutropenic patient is reassuring; a rising lactate is the earliest sign of deterioration before BP drops.  
4. **Piperacillin-tazobactam is king** – it covers *Pseudomonas*, anaerobes, and most Gram-positives. Only add vancomycin if suspected line infection, MRSA colonisation, or skin/soft tissue source.  
5. **Persistent fever >96h = think fungus.** Start caspofungin or voriconazole empirically; consider CT chest and abdomen.  
6. **Don’t stop antibiotics early.** Continue until ANC recovers to >0.5 (or >1.0 if still febrile). Usually 7–14 days for bacteraemia.  
7. **G-CSF is not for acute management.** It is for prophylaxis before the next cycle. In established sepsis, the inflammatory cytokines already raise neutrophil count; G-CSF may worsen capillary leak.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid**  
- **Using co-amoxiclav instead of piperacillin-tazobactam** – co-amoxiclav has poor anti-pseudomonal activity.  
- **Ordering a procalcitonin and delaying antibiotics** – no single test is sensitive enough to rule out bacterial infection in neutropenia.  
- **Assuming afebrile = safe** – check lactate and BP on every patient with neutropenia, even without fever.  
- **Forgetting to draw blood cultures from the central line** – you need differential time to positivity to diagnose line infection.  
- **Giving paracetamol for temperature** – masks fever and may hide response to treatment.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (registrar / consultant)**  
- Any neutropenic patient with fever ≥38°C (or hypothermia <36°C) – this is already a sepsis call.  
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** despite initial fluids.  
- **BP <90/60 or MAP <65** after 500 mL fluid bolus.  
- **GCS <15** or new confusion.  
- **Respiratory rate >25 / SpO2 <92% on room air.**  
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** (see below).

**Specific thresholds for ICU/crash team**  
- **NEWS2 ≥7** – immediate ICU referral.  
- **Lactate ≥4 mmol/L** (severe sepsis / septic shock) – transfer to ICU for central line, vasopressors, invasive monitoring.  
- **GCS ≤12** – airway protection risk; consider intubation.  
- **Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 hours** despite adequate fluids.  
- **Refractory hypotension** – not responding to 2 L crystalloid + noradrenaline >0.5 mcg/kg/min.

**NEWS2 scoring (quick reference):**  
| Parameter | Score 0 | Score 1 | Score 2 | Score 3 |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| RR | 12–20 | 9–11 | 21–24 | ≤8 or ≥25 |
| SpO2 | ≥96% | 94–95% | 92–93% | ≤91% |
| Temp | 36.1–38.0 | 35.1–36.0 or 38.1–39.0 | ≥39.1 | ≤35.0 |
| SBP | ≥110 | 100–109 | 90–99 | ≤89 |
| HR | 51–90 | 41–50 or 91–110 | 111–130 | ≤40 or ≥131 |
| Consciousness | Alert | | | New confusion / not alert |

**Remember:** Neutropenic sepsis can deteriorate faster than other sepsis because the lack of neutrophils prevents localisation. Any abnormal vital sign in a neutropenic patient is a red flag. Escalate early, treat early, and never assume it’s “just a fever.”
